title: Foul Smelling Discharge in Cat Skin Folds: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Noticed a bad smell in your cat's skin folds? Learn about skin fold dermatitis, why it happens, and when you need to contact your veterinarian.
content_html:
What is Skin Fold Discharge?

When moisture, heat, and friction are trapped within a cat's skin folds, it creates a breeding ground for bacteria and yeast. This condition is known as skin fold dermatitis, or intertrigo. The foul-smelling discharge you notice is typically a byproduct of these microorganisms overpopulating the area. This is most common in overweight cats or breeds with flat faces and extra skin, such as Persians.

Urgency Level: Medium

A foul-smelling discharge indicates an active infection that requires professional medical attention. While this is generally not an immediate life-threatening emergency, it is considered a medium-priority concern. You should schedule a veterinary appointment within the next 24 to 48 hours. Delaying treatment can lead to the infection deepening, causing significant pain, skin erosion, or the formation of an abscess.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Providing a high-quality photo to your veterinary team can be an invaluable triage tool. A clear image allows the vet to see the color and consistency of the discharge, as well as the level of redness and inflammation, before the area is disturbed during a physical exam. This helps them determine if the case is more urgent and prepares them to select the most appropriate diagnostic tests, such as skin cytology.

faqs.question: Can I clean the area with soap and water?
faqs.answer: While keeping the area clean is important, you should avoid using human soaps or harsh chemicals which can further irritate the skin. Use only water or vet-recommended antiseptic wipes until your appointment.

faqs.question: Is skin fold dermatitis painful for my cat?
faqs.answer: Yes, it can be very uncomfortable. The area is often itchy, tender, or stinging, which may cause your cat to over-groom or become sensitive when touched near the fold.

faqs.question: Will my cat need antibiotics?
faqs.answer: In many cases, yes. Depending on whether the infection is bacterial or fungal, your vet may prescribe topical ointments, medicated wipes, or oral medications to clear the infection.
